metadata.summary: Controlled experiment comparing two variants with 50/50 traffic split, measured against a pre-registered primary metric and statistical significance to make causal decisions.

content.title: A/B Testing Basics
content.summary: A/B testing (split testing) is a controlled experiment that compares two versions of a change by splitting traffic 50/50, measuring which variant performs better on a pre-registered primary metric, and deciding based on statistical significance. The lifecycle is: hypothesis (if/then/because) → sample-size calculation → run to full size → analyze (p-value, SRM check, guardrails) → ship or kill.

why.p: Opinion-driven product decisions fail because individual intuition has no error rate. A/B tests produce a causal estimate of a change's effect with a known false-positive rate (alpha). Without a pre-committed end date and a sample-ratio mismatch check, peeking inflates false positives 5–10x and SRM silently invalidates results.

list.item: Reversible UI/copy/flow changes where the change affects a metric trackable in real time.
At least 1k weekly events on the primary metric surface.
One change per experiment — multiple simultaneous changes prevent causal isolation.
Team has committed to a single primary metric and 3+ guardrail metrics before launch.
A/B platform available with deterministic-hash bucketing (user_id + experiment_id).
Strategic choices (pricing model, brand positioning) — blast radius too large, too few decisions.
Network-effect surfaces (Slack workspaces, marketplaces) — treatment leaks into control; use cluster/switchback designs.
Traffic below 100 users/day on the test surface — test duration exceeds product-iteration cadence.
Full redesigns — too many confounders; use phased feature-flag rollout instead.
Highly personalized products where every user already sees a different variant.

rule.statement: Write every hypothesis in if/then/because format before any code is written.
rule.rationale: Pre-registration prevents HARKing (Hypothesizing After Results are Known) and anchors the team to a single primary metric.

example.note: "The new design is better" — no metric, no mechanism, cannot be falsified.
IF we simplify signup from 6 fields to 3, THEN conversion will increase 10%, BECAUSE friction decreases.

rule.statement: Progress through all six steps in order; do not begin analysis before the pre-committed sample size and end date are reached.
rule.rationale: Peeking at interim results and stopping early inflates false positives by 5–10x.

list.item: 1. HYPOTHESIS — if/then/because, primary metric, guardrails, MDE
2. DESIGN — one change only; define control and treatment
3. SAMPLE SIZE — calculate n per variant using statsmodels NormalIndPower
4. RUN — random assignment, no peeking, simultaneous variants
5. ANALYZE — SRM check first, then z-test primary, then guardrails
6. DECIDE — ship, kill, retest, or investigate tradeoffs

rule.statement: Require raw counts (n1, x1, n2, x2) before computing significance; never accept a reported p-value without verifying the inputs.
rule.rationale: LLMs and junior analysts fabricate plausible-looking p-values when given only percentages.

list.item: p < 0.01 — strong evidence, ship B
0.01 to 0.05 — good evidence, ship B
0.05 to 0.10 — weak evidence, retest with larger sample
p > 0.10 — not significant, keep A

rule.statement: Treat 0.05 < p < 0.10 as "no result, need more data" — not as "almost a win".
rule.rationale: Rounding borderline results to wins doubles the realized false-positive rate over an experiment program.

list.item: B wins significantly → ship B to 100% via phased rollout (10% → 50% → 100%)
A wins significantly → keep A, document what failed and why
No significant difference → keep A (simpler), test a different hypothesis
B wins primary but hurts a guardrail → investigate tradeoffs before shipping

rule.statement: Choose exactly ONE primary metric per test; declare guardrail metrics as "watch only" — they cannot win or lose the test alone.
rule.rationale: Multiple primaries inflate the family-wise error rate; guardrails exist to catch Pyrrhic victories.

list.item: Landing page test → primary: signup conversion rate
Checkout flow test → primary: purchase completion rate
Email test → primary: click rate (not open rate — iOS prefetch noise)
Onboarding test → primary: activation rate within 7 days

section.name: Pre-Launch Gates

rule.statement: Block test launch if any of these six items is absent: hypothesis_if_then_because, primary_metric, mde_relative, sample_per_variant, end_date, guardrails.
rule.rationale: Tests missing any one of these cannot be analyzed correctly or compared to prior experiments in the log.

section.reference: Python function that enforces all six pre-launch gates and returns a pass/fail list

list.item: hypothesis written in if/then/because format
primary metric tied to a business outcome (not a vanity metric)
MDE stated as relative percentage (e.g. 10% relative lift on a 5% baseline)
sample_per_variant >= 1000 (below this, results are unreliable)
end_date committed in writing before traffic flows
3+ guardrail metrics named explicitly
no overlapping test on the same surface or user segment

rule.statement: Configure a sample-ratio mismatch (SRM) check on day 1; if observed split deviates from expected (chi-square p < 0.001), pause and investigate before proceeding.
rule.rationale: SRM indicates randomization failure and silently invalidates the entire test — an SRM-affected result cannot be trusted even if the primary metric looks significant.

list.item: verify tracking fires on both variants
run chi-square SRM test on observed traffic split
check that partial first/last days with ramp-up traffic are excluded from analysis window
1. SRM check (chi-square on split) — invalidate if p < 0.001
2. Primary metric z-test with raw counts n1, x1, n2, x2
3. Confidence interval on the lift
4. Secondary metrics (informational only)
5. All guardrail metrics — flag any regression > 5%
6. Segment check (day-of-week, device, acquisition channel) for heterogeneity

antipattern.description: Running analysis before the pre-committed sample size is reached.
antipattern.reason: Peeking inflates false positives — the test is not valid until the pre-registered sample and end date are both met.

antipattern.description: Testing 5 segments and calling the one that wins the "result".
antipattern.reason: Multiple comparisons without Bonferroni correction produces a ~23% family-wise false-positive rate at 5 tests.

example.note: Hypothesis: IF we change CTA from "Sign Up" to "Start Free Trial", THEN conversion will increase 8%, BECAUSE it emphasizes the free offer. Baseline 4.5%, MDE 8% relative (~0.36pp absolute), n=50k/variant, 3 weeks. Result: A=4.50% (n=52340), B=4.90% (n=51890), diff=+0.40pp (+8.9% relative), p=0.003 (99.7% confidence). Decision: ship B.

Hypothesis: IF we add "Most Popular" badge to Pro plan, THEN Pro selection will increase 15%, BECAUSE social proof influences choice. Result: A=15.0% (n=8450), B=17.0% (n=8380), diff=+2.0pp (+13.3%), p=0.048 (95.2% confidence). Decision: ship B — positive direction with sufficient confidence, even though below 15% MDE target.

Hypothesis: IF we add testimonials above the fold, THEN conversions will increase 10%, BECAUSE social proof builds trust. Result: A=5.00% (n=12000), B=4.79% (n=12200), diff=-0.21pp (-4.2%), p=0.32 (68% confidence). Decision: keep A (no significance, and directionally negative). Learning: testimonials may have distracted from the CTA; test placement below the fold instead.

rule.statement: When a test fails directionally, document the specific learning, not just "hypothesis was wrong" — failed tests are the most informative entries in the experiment log.
rule.rationale: The experiment log is the cumulative knowledge base for the growth function; vague failure entries cannot inform future test design.

antipattern.description: Peeking at interim results and stopping early when a winner appears.
antipattern.reason: Inflates false positives 5–10x; the test is only valid at the pre-committed sample size and end date.

antipattern.description: Testing multiple simultaneous changes in one variant.
antipattern.reason: Cannot isolate which change caused the effect; the test produces an uninterpretable compound result.

antipattern.description: Ignoring guardrail metrics after a primary win.
antipattern.reason: Pyrrhic victories — e.g. a signup-rate win that increases D7 churn — destroy more value than they create.

template.purpose: Python gate function — blocks underpowered or misconfigured tests before launch
LLM prompt to draft a hypothesis card with all required fields
